1: The divine speech formula vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe opens the section on the menorah. Vayedaber (from dabar, H1696, to speak) is the standard legislative introduction; its repetition throughout Leviticus and Numbers structures the entire Sinai revelation as a series of divine addresses.
2: Behaalotekha (be-ha-alotekha, from alah, H5927): ‘when you cause to go up’ or ‘when you mount.’ This is the title of the Torah portion. The same root gives ‘olah (H5930), the burnt offering that ‘goes up’ in smoke to YHWH. The verb is also used for ascending to the sanctuary, going up to battle, and the sun rising. The lamps that ‘go up’ toward the menorah face (meul penei ha-menorah, ‘toward the face of the lampstand’) share their root with the offering that ascends entirely to God.
3: Aaron does ken (ken, H3651, thus/so): the compliance formula signals perfect obedience. El-mul penei ha-menorah = toward the face of the lampstand; penei (construct of panim, H6440) = face. The lamps illuminate ‘toward the face’ — the directional language of divine encounter permeates the sanctuary.
4: Miqdash (ha-menorah miqdash): miqdash (H4720) = sanctuary/holy place, from qadash (H6942) = to be holy/set apart. The menorah is described as made according to the pattern (mareh, H4758) that YHWH showed Moses. The pattern-correspondence between the heavenly original and the earthly copy is a recurring concern in the tabernacle construction (Ex 25:9, 40).
5: Another vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe opens the Levite consecration section. The two-part structure of ch.8 — menorah (vv.1-4) then Levites (vv.5-26) — may be intentional: the lampstand provides light for the sanctuary; the Levites provide the human service-structure that maintains it.
6: Qach et-ha-Leviyim = ‘take the Levites’ (laqach, H3947, to take/bring). Vetiharta otam = ‘and purify them’ (taher, H2891, Piel = to cleanse/purify). The Piel intensification of taher indicates a formal, deliberate purification process.
7: Mei chattat = literally ‘waters of purification offering’ or ‘sin-water.’ Chattat (H2403) is the same term used for the sin/purification offering; here applied to the sprinkling water. Ve-he'eviru ta'ar al-kol-besaram = ‘let a razor pass over all their flesh’ (ta'ar, H8593); total body-hair removal signals the Levites’ complete consecration, analogous to the shaving of the healed skin disease sufferer (Lev 14:8-9).
8: Par ben-baqar = ‘a bull, a son of the herd’ (par, H6499; baqar, H1241). The ‘son of the herd’ specifies a young adult male, the most costly sacrificial animal category. Solet (solet, H5560) = fine flour for the minchah grain offering accompanying the burnt offering. The dual offering (bull for ‘olah; bull for chattat) mirrors the high priest’s consecration (Lev 8:14-21).
9: The Levites are brought near (hiqravta, from qarav, H7126, to bring near/approach) before the tent of meeting, then the whole congregation (kol-adat) assembles. The entire community’s participation in the Levitical consecration signals that the Levites represent all Israel, not merely the priestly class.
10: Samekhu venei-Yisrael et-yedeihem al-ha-Leviyim = ‘the children of Israel shall lay their hands on the Levites.’ Samakh (H5564, Qal) = to lean/lay upon. This is the same hand-laying gesture used in sacrifice (Lev 1:4; 3:2) when the offerer identifies with the animal. Israel’s hand-laying on the Levites makes the substitution structural and visible: the Levites stand before YHWH as Israel.
11: Tenufah (tenufah, H8573): wave/elevation offering; from nuf (H5130) = to wave, move to and fro. Aaron ‘waves’ the Levites as a tenufah before YHWH. Applying this term to persons is unique — it normally denotes portions of animal sacrifice (Lev 7:30). The Levites are formally presented as a human offering from Israel to YHWH.
12: The Levites lay hands on the bull’s heads: ve-samekhu ha-Leviyim et-yadeihem al-rosh ha-parim. The chain of substitution: Israel lays hands on Levites (v.10), Levites lay hands on bulls (v.12) = Israel → Levites → bulls. Kipper al-ha-Leviyim = ‘make atonement for the Levites’ (kipper, H3722, Piel = to atone/cover/wipe).
13: The Levites stand before Aaron and his sons and are waved (henafta otam tenufah) before YHWH a second time. The repetition of the wave-offering action (vv.11, 13, 15, 21) emphasizes that the Levites’ status as a human offering to YHWH is permanently established.
14: Vehivdaltem et-ha-Leviyim miqerev benei-Yisrael = ‘you shall separate the Levites from among the children of Israel.’ Hivdil (from badal, H914, Hiphil) = to separate, distinguish. The same verb separates light from darkness (Gen 1:4), clean from unclean (Lev 20:25), and Israel from the nations. The Levites’ separation is a microcosm of Israel’s own separation.
15: Avodah (avodah, H5656): service, work, labor; from abad (H5647) = to serve/work/worship. The same word covers slave-labor, agricultural work, and liturgical service. Tahor (tahor, H2889) = clean/pure: after purification, the Levites are clean and ready for service.
16: Netunm netunm hem li = ‘given, given they are to me.’ The doubled netun (from natan, H5414 = to give) is emphatic: the Levites are wholly given. Takhat kol-peter rechem = ‘in place of all who open the womb’ (peter, H6363 = firstborn-opener); the Levites substitute for all firstborns of Israel.
17: Ki li kol-bechor = ‘for every firstborn is mine.’ YHWH’s ownership claim on the firstborn is the foundational premise of Levitical theology: the Levites exist as the corporate redemption-substitute for every firstborn Israelite, based on the Exodus night (Ex 13:2). The Levites carry the meaning of the Exodus firstborn-deliverance forward.
18: Ettenu et-ha-Leviyim tachat kol-bechor benei-Yisrael = ‘I give the Levites instead of all firstborns.’ Tachat (H8478) = under/instead of/in exchange for. The substitution root: lex talionis (nefesh tachat nefesh), the ram instead of Isaac (Gen 22:13), the Levites instead of the firstborns. Substitution is structural throughout the covenant economy.
19: Le-khapper al-benei-Yisrael = ‘to make atonement for the children of Israel.’ Negef (negef, H5063): plague, blow, striking; from nagaf (H5062) = to strike/smite. The Levites’ mediating atonement-function prevents the negef that would occur if unauthorized persons approached the sanctuary. The same word describes the plagues of Egypt and the deaths from sanctuary transgression (Num 16:47).
20: Full compliance formula: Moses, Aaron, and all the congregation did to the Levites ke-kol asher-tzivah YHWH et-Moshe = ‘according to all that YHWH commanded Moses.’ The full-community compliance notation marks the Levitical consecration as a formally complete covenant act witnessed by the whole assembly.
21: Vayitchattau ha-Leviyim va-yechabsu bigdeihem = ‘and the Levites purified themselves and washed their garments.’ Chata in the Hithpael (H2398) = to purify oneself; the reflexive form of the purification-offering verb. Washing garments (kibbes, H3526) is a standard purification step (Lev 11:25; 13:6; 14:8-9; 15:5-27).
22: Avodatam = their service (avodah, H5656 + possessive). The Levites now enter their service under Aaron and his sons. The hierarchy is explicit: Levites serve the sanctuary under priestly supervision; priests serve YHWH directly. This two-tier structure reflects the increasing degrees of holiness toward YHWH’s presence.
23: Another divine speech formula opens the age-limit section. The specificity of the service regulations (ages 25-50) reflects the administrative reality of a tribe numbered for service: not everyone serves at peak capacity at every stage of life, and the liturgical infrastructure requires managed succession.
24: Latzava tzava (from tzava, H6635, H6633) = ‘to do military/conscript service.’ Tzava means both ‘army/host’ and the ‘mustering/service’ of that host. The Levitical service is described as a tzava — a military conscription into YHWH’s service — from age 25. Israel is organized as YHWH’s military camp.
25: Yashav me-tzva ha-avodah = ‘shall cease from the mustering of the service’ at age 50. Yashav (H3427) = to sit, dwell, retire from; the Levite who has passed his prime ‘sits down’ from heavy carrying work. This does not mean total retirement — v.26 specifies ongoing lighter assistance.
26: Vesharet = ‘and he shall minister’ (sharat, H8334, Piel = to attend, minister; used almost exclusively for priestly/Levitical service and royal attendants). The 50+ Levite may still sharet his brothers but shall not carry heavy loads. Avodah lo ya'avod = ‘service he shall not serve’ — the cognate absolute emphasizes the absoluteness of the restriction on heavy service.
